beats.prompt: You've seen a fair game's net gain before. Match each scenario to its expected net.

pairs.left: E[total net], sum of fair bets

feedback.correct: A sum of fair bets is still fair — that's linearity in disguise.
hints: Tap a left card, then its match on the right.
A fair game has E[net] = 0 by definition.
Match: one fair bet → 0; sum of fair bets → 0.

beats.prompt: There are 100 noodles in a bowl. Blindfolded, you grab two loose ends at random and tie them, again and again. How many loops do you expect?

About 50.note: Let's test it — that's near half the noodles; each tie rarely closes a loop, so loops stay few.

About 10.note: Let's test it — closer, but still high; the tiny per-tie chances add to about three loops.

Just a handful.note: Good instinct — let's prove it by adding each tie's small chance of closing a loop.

beats.prompt: In L1 you built the full {2…12} pmf to find E[two dice] = 7. Using linearity, get the same answer in one line. What is E[X + Y] when X and Y are fair dice?

feedback.correct: Exactly — linearity lets you add the dice averages without touching the joint distribution. E[X+Y] = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7.
hints: No need to rebuild the sum's pmf. Add each die's average: 3.5 + 3.5 = 7.
Each fair die has E = 7/2 = 3.5. Linearity says E[X+Y] = E[X] + E[Y].

beats.prompt: Press Step to tie a pair of ends. Watch the running E[loops] build up — one tiny bet at a time.

feedback.correct: Each tie adds one tiny loop-closing chance — the running total stays shockingly small.
hints: Press Step to tie one pair of ends.
Watch E[loops] after each tie.
After 100 noodles, the total is only ≈ 3.28.

beats.interviewNote: The product rule E[XY]=E[X]E[Y] fails for dependent variables. The sum rule E[X+Y]=E[X]+E[Y] never fails — it is a property of summation itself, not of independence. The first-ace decomposition (53/5) is a linearity sum only; why each piece=1/5 is the indicator argument, owned by L3.
beats.prompt: Tap each card to reveal three facets of linearity — the rule, a decomposition, and the #1 confusion.

lenses.label: The Rule
lenses.body: E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] — the expectation of a sum is the sum of the expectations, whether or not X and Y are independent. (Green Book p.47)

lenses.label: First Ace
lenses.body: E[wait for first ace] = 1 + 48·(1/5) = 53/5 ≈ 10.6. Each card position contributes a tiny indicator expectation. Sum 49 tiny bets — no joint distribution needed. (Why each piece = 1/5 is L3's job.)

lenses.label: ⚠️ Product Rule
lenses.body: E[XY] = E[X]·E[Y] ONLY when X and Y are independent. The SUM rule has no such restriction — this is the #1 confusion between the two rules.

feedback.correct: Linearity adds; the sum rule ignores dependence. The product rule is the one with the independence fine print.
hints: Reveal each card in turn.
Card 3 holds the #1 confusion.
Sum rule: always adds. Product rule: needs independence.

beats.prompt: Quick clarification before we use the sum rule on dependent noodle ties — read the card.

interaction.title: Sum rule vs Product rule
interaction.body:
The SUM rule needs no independence; only the PRODUCT rule does.

• E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] — always true, dependent or not.
• E[XY] = E[X]·E[Y] — only when X and Y are independent.

The noodle ties are dependent (each tie changes the pool), yet the sum rule still works.

feedback.correct: Got it — add expectations freely, regardless of dependence.
hints: E[X+Y]=E[X]+E[Y] — unconditional.
E[XY]=E[X]E[Y] — independence required.
The noodle ties are dependent, yet the sum still works.

beats.prompt: Track A scaffold: walk through the per-tie probability for n=2 noodles.

interaction.title: Per-tie loop probability (n = 2)
interaction.body:
With n noodles, after k−1 ties there are 2(n−k+1) loose ends.

P(loop at k-th tie) = 1 / (2(n−k+1) − 1)

For n = 2:
• k=1 (4 ends remain): P = 1/(4−1) = 1/3
• k=2 (2 ends remain): P = 1/(2−1) = 1

E[loops] = 1/3 + 1 = 4/3 — even though tie 2 depends on tie 1.

feedback.correct: The pool shrinks each tie — but linearity still adds each small chance.
hints: n=2: 4 ends → P(loop, first tie) = 1/3.
After that tie, 2 ends remain → P(loop, second tie) = 1.

beats.prompt: Two noodles, four loose ends. You make two random ties. What is E[loops]? (Enter a fraction like 4/3.)

feedback.correct: Correct — even though tie 2 depends on tie 1, linearity adds them directly: 1/3 + 1 = 4/3.
hints: Dependence doesn't block linearity. Add each tie's loop-closing chance: 1 + 1/3 = 4/3.
First tie: P(loop) = 1/(2·2−1) = 1/3. Second tie (2 ends remain): P(loop) = 1/(2·1−1) = 1.

beats.interviewNote: E[loops]=Σ_{k=1}^{n}1/(2k−1)≈½ln(2n) for large n — sub-logarithmic growth. For n=100: ≈3.28. Linearity holds because each 1/(2k−1) is the expectation of one indicator, regardless of what earlier ties did. Compare: E[loops,n=100]/100≈0.033 — not even 1 loop per 30 noodles.
beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: three noodles, six loose ends, three ties. Use E[loops] = Σ 1/(2k−1) to find E[loops] for n = 3. Enter your answer as a fraction.

interaction.scenario: Each tie closes a loop with probability 1/(2(n−k+1)−1). For n=3 the three terms are 1/5, 1/3, 1. Sum them — and you'll see exactly why 100 noodles stays near 3.28.

feedback.correct: n=3: 1 + 1/3 + 1/5 = 23/15. For 100 noodles ≈ 3.28 — logarithmic growth, not proportional.
hints: Loops don't scale with noodles. Add the odd-denominator chances: 1 + 1/3 + 1/5 = 23/15.

feedback.correct: Break the tangle into a sum, add the easy pieces — dependence doesn't matter. Next: why does each piece equal 1/(2k−1)? That's the indicator variable trick in L3.
hints: E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] — always.
Dependence doesn't block the sum rule.
100 noodles ≈ 3.28 loops. The next lesson explains each piece.
